title: AdaCuRL: Adaptive Curriculum Reinforcement Learning with Invalid Sample Mitigation and Historical Revisiting
content:
AdaCuRL: Adaptive Curriculum Reinforcement Learning with Invalid Sample Mitigation and Historical Revisiting# GRPO may face two fatal problems:
When all answers in a group have the same reward, the train signal may be trivial.
In this case, the KL regulation term will dominate the loss. The model will be rollbacked to the old version. A “wrapper” of GRPO can solve these problems.
Concepts
Curriculum Learning: a technique inspired by human learning. Given a dataset, the model will learn knowledge from easy to hard to achieve better performance and faster convergence. Formally, given dataset $$D$$:
$$D = {(x_i, y_i, d_i)}|_{i = 1} ^N, \text{and the difficulties, } d_i \text{, is ordered increasingly.} $$
Self-Paced Learning: we assume that loss can directly reflect the difficulties of problems. We use loss as a measure of difficulty and set a threshold to limit the difficulty of the set. Formally:
$$D = {(x_i, y_i)| l(\hat y, y) \le \tau }, \text{where } \tau \text{ is the threshold of the selection.}$$
That is to say, only QA pairs with loss less than $$\tau$$can be added to the dataset.
We will dynamically increase or decrease $$\tau$$based on the model’s performance
Coarse-to-Fine Difficulty Estimation in AdaCuRL
Coarse:
Given a dataset, let the model produce five responses for each question. Count the number of correct responses. Let $$c$$be the number of correct answers, $$c_i \in {0, 1,2,3,4,5}$$.
Create three bins to hold the questions with the corresponding # of correct responses:
$$G_1 = {i | c_i \in [0, 1]} \ G_2 = {i | c_i \in [2, 3]} \ G_3 = {i | c_i \in [4, 5]} $$
Sample from the three bins: $$S = \bigcup_{k = 1}^3 \text{Sample}(G_k, \lfloor \rho_k|G_k| \rfloor)$$, where $$\rho_k$$ is the predefined sampling ratio for k-th bin.
Fine: For each question in $$S$$, generate $$N$$answers, where $$N»5$$. Let $$c(q)$$be the # of correct of each question, $$\text{difficulty}(q) = 1 - \frac{c(q)}{N}$$.
Filter out the question with difficulty higher than 0.95 or less than 0.05 to ensure the stability of the train. Sorted by difficulties, the result dataset is $$D$$.
Curriculum Reinforcement Learning in AdaCuRL
We partition $$D$$into $$K$$buckets: $$B_k|_{k = 1}^{K}$$with equal size but increasing difficulty. At the beginning, we use $$B_1$$as the trainset $$D_c$$ and gradually merge the following buckets into $$D_c$$.
To determine whether we update $$D_c$$or not, we maintain a $$cs$$variable, initialized to 0, to indicate the competence score $$\in [0, 1]$$.
The update rule is:
$$cs_{t+1} = cs_t+(\bar r - 0.5)\max(1-cs_t, \gamma)$$, where
$$\bar r$$is the average reward.
$$\gamma$$is a hyperparameter to avoid the rate being too small.
When $$cs $$reaches a ratio: $$cs \ge \frac{k-1}{K}$$, we update $$D_c$$. This works as $$k$$is related to the difficulty: larger $$k$$= higher difficulty. Intuitively, when the model cannot yet fully understand current $$D_c$$, its $$\bar r$$will be small (can be less than 0.5), so the framework will force the model to spend more time on questions from current $$D_c$$. Once it is mastered, $$\bar r$$is large, and $$\bar r - 0.5$$ will be positive, it increases $$cs$$until it reaches the threshold.
After merging, we reset $$cs = \frac{k-1}{K}$$.
Reward Function: (This framework is for binary tasks) The reward has two parts: format reward and accuracy reward.
The reward serves two purposes: Core GRPO update (group reward -> average -> calculate advantage)
Decide when to update the current trainset $$D_c$$.
GRPO-related modification
Conditional KL to avoid model degrading: $$L_{GRPO} = - \mathbb{E}_i[\min(\rho_i\cdot \hat A_i,\ \text{CLIP}\cdot \hat A_i)]+ \beta \bold{I[\hat A_i \ne 0]} \mathbb{E}_i[KL(\cdot)]$$
It’s to say, when the advantage is 0, we don’t calculate KL to keep the model untouched. Self-pacing Mechanism
We update $$D$$after some training. Use the updated model to re-evaluate the difficulty of the problems and eliminate those being “too easy”.
$$D’ ={q \in D | \text{new difficulty}_q \ge \tau’}$$
Re-divide $$D’$$to buckets and continue the training. Algorithm Pseudocodes:
